title: REVEALED! The World's Favourite Dermal Filler Brand - Survey Results [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: REVEALED! The World_s Favourite Dermal Filler Brand - Survey Results [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: If i met a patient who said yeah i've been making my own hyaluronic acid in the basement and but i'm sick of doing that will you do it for me i'd be like sorry because i have no idea what's in your skin welcome to the ascetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce …

segments.text: if i met a patient who said yeah i've been making my own hyaluronic acid in the basement and but i'm sick of doing that will you do it for me i'd be like sorry because i have no idea what's in your skin welcome to the ascetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we've got some really exciting results from our first survey where we're going to look at the distribution of filler choice across the globe so we've got some really interesting data about which fillers you guys are using then we're going to discuss how you should choose your filler how you actually choose your filler and what i suggest we look at in terms of reactivity and different filler types so this all came from the kobe vaccine and people saying that the well three people had reacted they had filler presence and so you thought let's get some data and this is the first tranche of the data isn't it yeah so we're going to survey entire list and work through other people as well so hopefully get as big a

data set as we can and we're going to try and find out um what proportion of different fillers are reacting so if we've got this very unusual very valuable situation where we know a lot of people are going to be exposed at a set point in time to an inflammatory situation which is what all vaccines do and then we can measure reactivity but you can't do that unless you've got a list of all different products people having now one of the things that first frustrated me about that initial report is we have no idea how many people in that study of the vaccine had dermal filler in their system we have no idea what filler was in the system and then caused the reaction and we have no idea about the medical history of those patients who actually had the vaccine so what we're going to do is try and do our bit to try and sort some of that out so that we can hopefully get a little bit of insight into what are the factors that predispose people to reactivity and as we said last week i need your help for that so do get involved there should be a link with some more information below this video the first link that we put through last week though was we asked you guys what filler you used and we found out some juicy information yeah so we're going to go

through what the world's most popular fillers are and what you guys are actually using in the clinic and see if we can see any interesting insights between the type of filler and the volume of injection injecting that people are doing because um all of this should help you choose wisely with your product choice and then we'll also talk about how i think you should choose your filler so tell us about the survey so we got managed to get responses from over 52 different countries which i'm really pleased about when you look at the map it's most of the globe's surface apart from the oceans which is pretty cool and then we got 2106 responses and obviously we are predominantly uk heavy so 60 of those responses were in the uk and that might help you interpreting this if you live elsewhere um but there is definitely some interesting data coming from it and if you want to dive in deeper we are making all of this raw data available in the link down below and if you want to create some content out of it don't forget to share us maybe i'll even have a discussion with anyone who comes up with any interesting insights just inbox us and let us know so tell us how the filler categories broke down

broke down so the question we asked is which is your favorite filler and we got hundreds of different responses thousands of different responses and many small niche products which we haven't listed we're starting at the cutoff of one percent popularity and that is radiesse starting out as non-hyaluronic based product um and that's the only one that is non-hyaluronic acid based in this survey result next on the list is versa and that represented two percent of responders then we have stylage at four percent belatero at six percent relax at ten percent wrestling covering sixteen percent of responses tsl at seventeen percent and then allagan if you take the company as a whole is 30 but you can divide that up into juvederm ultra at 10 percent and vicross technology at 20 percent so the most popular filler by volume in our survey was the vikros range of dermal filler products so obviously the majority of people who responded were

responded were uk based so a filler like versa for example which is made by pallenium in canada i'm sure if we asked just the canadians that would be more popular yeah well there were certainly some interesting things we'll get into in terms of the volume of use so we looked at how much volume each brand was being used so if a clinician favored a particular product how much were they injecting in an average week and versa was represented quite highly and some of those in some of those more niche numbers so it was one that where there were clinics using huge amounts essentially with that brand and that's actually not true with some of the less common brands in the rest of the survey and i think there's something in that which is i think it's it probably fits a different niche when you look at it in from a north american context than what we have more european centralized and i'm sure they're working hard to change that what did we learn about the volume of filler people are buying so it's not it's no big surprise that the bigger accounts dominate the brands the more famous brands so um 50 of the biggest accounts were allegan

so that's juvederm and juvederm via cross and then we had um represented as well with one clinic each uh teoxane galderma fox farmer which with revelax and mertz so um that it's an interesting distribution at that higher end uh which is worth thinking about so what you mean by that is that the people who were purchasing between five thousand and ten thousand miller filler per month they were with the big filler brands yeah which could we could hypothesize why that is and whether it's a rational choice or whether that's the support they get from the team whether it's a sales effort by the companies themselves who know the big accounts are whether it's experience that makes you choose certain products whether it's just the cost of the product and there are many different factors that come into play okay so let's dive into if someone's watching us here and they may be quite new to aesthetics or they're thinking about changing brands how can we make a great decision about which one of these brands

to choose well that it's really difficult because if you were going to make a purely clinical decision you'd need a lot of data and you'd need a table that compares every different characteristic and then you would choose a product that best matched the need that you had for that product so for example you'd have um you know the cohesivity of the product and there'd be a number that you could look at and you compare it to the other products and you'd know that you want a high cos cohesivity product for some areas and maybe something softer for others you know about reaction rates for example you'd know 0.5 chance of this product reacting for example you would simply look through that table and choose the safest most effective longest lasting most predictable product and then you would use it now unfortunately we are miles away from that is not how anyone chooses products because it's incredibly hard to even get close to that table and the data would be very hard to get um i don't think for example is g prime is spoken about quite a lot as a desirable feature but we don't actually know what is in the tissues like we know what it is in a

test tube but it's completely different in the tissues the interaction between the molecules and the tissues will completely change their products and so it becomes about experience i know certain products that when i put in a certain amount it seems to project out in a way that's better than other products and it's a it's an experiential thing that i really firmly believe but that's not science and if we were thinking about complications again what kind of questions might we ask ourselves to decide which filler to get yeah so um there are lots of different variables you would look at if in terms of minimizing the risk of complications so firstly you'd say is it dissolvable regardless of what the molecule is can you get it out quickly if you need to you might also want to be looking at in terms of the safety process does it aspirate well because some products it doesn't matter how long you pull back on the plunger you will never get blood back in and that is another feature that you might want to think about when choosing your product if it aspirates well but you can still cause an occlusion obviously you need to know does it dissolve well so there's another feature we just don't know like how

much hyaluronidase does it take to dissolve every filler and these are some of the many questions i would love to get answers uh about over time um but you're looking for an ideal product but there's always a payoff like you never just get one factor without paying somewhere else i'll tell you one example of that which is definition versus volume if you have a really good product at creating definition you usually can't create as much volume with it and if you get something is really great volume is usually not so good at creating definition and there's always this trade-off and that is true with all these characteristics and of course the patient is very important deciding factor as well so that you can just consult them and decide what will be right for them yeah well for the patient often it's about cost and they don't understand these factors anywhere near in as much detail for the clinician sometimes is about how nice it is to inject so do you have good control over your syringe or is it i mean there are certain products i've used which are you have to push very hard to get it going and sometimes the

most annoying feature would be that there's an inertia in the syringe so you're pushing and nothing happens and then suddenly it gives there are a couple of products i've used over the years that are a bit like that and that for me that's just intolerable so even if that was perfect in every other way i can't sculpt with that because it's too unpredictable it would be so lovely if we just say this is the best filler but there are and i haven't even touched the surface of how many different characteristics you might look at um but you should attempt i think safety would be first um and then you want to have a sense of the predictability like if i create this today what will it look like in three months time and that is you can only get through experience i believe when you ask someone on a forum which filler do you recommend we know from our survey the majority of people use very small amounts but more than 50 of our surgery of our survey use less than 10. so if you're getting per month so if you're getting advice on a forum you'll get probably statistically likely to be getting advice from people who use smaller amounts and that doesn't mean that their advice

is wrong but they certainly they definitely have less experience and also there are many new injectors in the uk so they don't they haven't been injecting for 10 years i probably anyone who's injecting only 10 a month has not been injecting for 10 years so the advice is based on what it's like on the day when i inject does it feel nice to inject and do the lips look like i wanted them to that's a very small part of what makes up the filler the most important part is actually what happens after that how does this product behave will it react will it cause a complication later can i easily reverse it will it diffuse or emigrate migrate from where you've placed it um will it still be there in three years time causing a problem will it disappear in three months time you know it's the long-term behavior that actually makes more of a difference i think so patience and to patient safety than how it looks on the day and unfortunately that's very hard data to get in fact what you mainly get is experience and when you talk to an experienced clinician it's very hard to get experienced in 10 different fellow

brands you tend to get experienced in one villa brand and so it's incredibly hard to make a digital decision you end up making an emotional decision which one are you familiar with which one does your friend use that you trust uh which one have you heard of you know which one is fda approved will i do advocate for that in the uk because you know there's some data behind it um which one do you use so we use primarily vi cross juvederm cross and some juvederm as well i would only use vicross if i could because i mean i just mean in terms of the other juvederm range because i feel it's got that really good balance between definition and volume and i know i can it retains definition over time and can we mix fillers in one phase um there's no data to say there's a problem with that so it most people will make a first principle decision which is these fillers are so similar that it's very unlikely that there's going to be interaction they're basically mainly hyaluronic acid and even if it's not hyaluronic acid is there any chemical reaction that you

can think of probably not so without data you make a first principle decision that is likely to be safe i heard that there were some insurance companies were said that you should wait a certain number of months yeah some people i've heard people say that now what we know from interviewing dr martin from his studies if you ultrasound people even three years after most people got phil are still in their system even though they will say they can't see it and i'm not surprised by this at all that was my clinical experience long before i had the proof from dr master is that these fillers last much longer so for me it doesn't make it it's not rational to say you should wait six months before you mix filler because you probably should never treat that patient again if you want a clean slate with every patient and you want to only have one filler in their face it's just not practical for most of us if you happen to be one of these and i have heard extremely well known injectors saying they won't treat anyone who's had treatment elsewhere now most of us do not have that luxury to be rejecting 50 of your patients you know if you have a cue and a waiting list three years long maybe you can do that but most of

us have to be reasonable and say and make a risk assessment is there a known problem no could there be a problem possibly but is it likely based on what you know about those products no if there is a problem what are you going to do to solve it well we're going to use hyaluronidase if it's a hyaluronic acid filler it's very similar to whatever you're using on the day then i feel like it's reasonable to mix products um you know in a practical sense the other thing that we used to say and this is going about years is that you shouldn't mix products because if you did then if there was a problem the manufacturers would say well it's not my filigolf and then the other manufacturer would say well that's not mine filler how do you know but again i feel like that's maybe moved on a bit because i'm not sure that the manufacturers sort of do all that much to help in difficult situations anyway well the thing that you're really worried about in that situation is someone buying stuff off the internet or injecting themselves we've got an interesting case i came across of someone making up their own hyaluronic acid gel out of some powder that wasn't even met it was for topical use and they're injecting themselves

injecting themselves now if you're injecting someone who's done that you have no idea what's actually in their skin and you may get some sort of reactive process that's impossible to solve that causes horrible nodules that are permanent that start four months after you do your filler so if there's that degree of uncertainty if i met a patient who said yeah i've been making my own hyaluronic acid in the basement um but i'm sick of doing that will you do it for me i'd be like sorry because i have no idea what's in your skin and when you have a problem or if you have a problem i won't know what to do and we won't know if it's my product or yours and i'll be lost so for that reason i'm out of that situation um so that that's very different but i'm talking about a good patient who's gone to reasonable clinics who's had a different brand of product that is within the realm of risk i'm happy to take but maybe not to mix tapes of filler like radiesse sculptra hellonic acid well even that if you know what you're dealing with you can get around some of it with documentation now some of these products you know there is a different type of

reaction you may get with sculpture i've certainly seen a patient with reactive nodules i think it's less likely less common now than it used to be because i think they either change the formula or how it's administered but anyway if you've got a good documentation of where the filler is placed and sculpture is usually placed differently that's one of the one case we had we knew it wasn't ours because we had a good documentation of where our treatment was and where the reactive nodules were you could still but you're right it's into a different a slightly different realm of risk when you're mixing different categories of filler which may have different reactions particularly if that reaction is harder to solve you know a permanent nodule is a much worse problem and if you're injecting something that may cause a permanent problem in a year after your treatment um then you're taking a significantly bigger risk than if it's just hyaluronic acid the same as what you already use so how does the choice of filler affect the vaccine survey so if we're going to look at people reacting after the vaccine it's going to be very helpful to know what type of filler they've had and the reason for that is because not all

fillers are equal and what the main reason for that is that they're all made differently and they literally got different things in them and there has been some pretty high profile discussion around different products i know the vikros ranges had some question marks around whether it's more reactive or not and we actually audited all of our patients and found it was point four percent which was point five percent was what's predicted so i did not find that in my patient cohort but it's something you will see on social media all the time and a lot of high profile people will discuss it um what a great opportunity we've got to actually see if that's true with this particular exposure so that's why i'm interested in it we need to pin it down to different types but there are lots of components about filler and even the way it's administered that could also affect inflammation so although we'll get back some data it's not it won't be without further questions and never is isn't it more research required as they always say so what are the factors that affect reactions to filler well the way i see it is reactions to filler are broadly about an inflammatory

state so what are the things that cause inflammation in patients and those are probably going to be similar to the list of things that cause inflammation with filler now the type of fillers obviously going to make a difference too there is some interesting um evidence that short training hyaluronic acid fillers are or not hyaluronic acid fillers but short chain hyaluronic acids are pro-inflammatory they actually cause an inflammatory response but long-chain hyaluronic acids are actually anti-inflammatory so and this is just to do with the science that there are actually receptors within cells that detect hyaluronic acids and respond to them so um there is something about the actual molecule but there might also be something about how it's all glued together so you know all these fillers are made slightly differently we have the nasher technology and we have vitros technology and we have bdde and there are various uh com components that are added to hyaluronic acid that may cause a problem and there are also um the possibility of impurities you know they're all hydraulic gases are made by bacteria and then it's purified but there is a small chance that some will have a

higher concentration of bacterial cell walls which could cause reactivity in theory so it would be very weird if there was no difference between dermal filler brands i mean everything if you put something through a vastly different process or even a moderately different process it's likely that you're going to get a slightly different result um and that doesn't mean that one will be unsafe and some will be safe it just means there'll be little differences between the two i don't think you're going to find one that's 100 times different to the other i think they'll be within a few percentage of each other would be what i guess we'll find out um but that's because they're made differently so that's the actual product you've then of course got patience you've got to also recognize that if you have a patient who's already inflamed and then you inject them with the filler it's way more likely that they're going to become inflamed due to the filler so autoimmune disease allergies dental work can do it so if you have dental work you get a back to a temporary bacteremia so bacteria literally enters your bloodstream and your body responds to that and you get inflammation so having a dental

procedure might cause a reaction with your filler um having a viral infection and of course the thing we're interested in most having a vaccine these are all things that may trigger a response to the filler so we need to try and dissect what is related to the vaccine and the filler and what is related what are the other risk factors that inform that because we want to try and make sure that we can separate the other risk factors out and look at just those healthy patients with no risk factors who have a vaccine have filler in their system what is their chance of reacting i'd like to find out so guys don't forget to click on the link below and register your interest for the next wave of the survey there's some amazing data that we can get if we all pull together and i really need your help so click on the link register your interest and you will hear from us shortly thank you very much for watching the video and please don't forget to subscribe and hit the bell if you want to be notified when we go live with more amazing aesthetics mastery shows thank you for watching
